acceptance_targets: [shape_of_metaphor_tic_chapter_18] Pass if the revised material now follows the rewrite direction and no longer exhibits the described defect. Fail if a cold reader would still identify the same underlying problem.

[LW09-001] On a reread from Chapter 17 into Chapter 18, the first page of Chapter 18 should add new tactical information without restating the eight-hundred-meter problem, Osen's containment method, or the basic 'don't spend unless called' rule.

[LW09-002] A reader moving from snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:7847 through snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8484 should be able to chart one forward-moving clock without any reference that places the Chapter 18 chase earlier in the day than the Chapter 17 launch.

[LW09-003] From the ridgeline in Chapter 17 through the threshold in Chapter 18, a reader should be able to name the same four Reserve opponents and track their functions without ever having to assume an extra agent has appeared off-page.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised material now follows the rewrite direction and no longer exhibits the described defect. Fail if a cold reader would still identify the same underlying problem.
findings.evidence: Line 8270: 'Vel could almost admire the shape of it when she hated her enough.' Line 8602: 'The shape of the world started to slip'
findings.problem: Two instances in the climax chapter. Line 8270 uses 'shape' for tactical admiration (replaceable). Line 8602 describes the threshold's physical distortion and may be defensible as literal rather than formulaic.
findings.rewrite_direction: Audit all eleven instances. Retain at most three where the formula does genuine work: line 5564's contrastive pair ('Not the shape of a hope. The shape of a requirement.') earns its repetition through deliberate parallelism; line 8602's physical usage ('The shape of the world started to slip') may be defensible as literal description at the threshold moment. Replace the remaining instances with constructions native to their scene: physical metaphors, Ledger-vocabulary substitutions, or direct statement. In Chapter 13, reduce from three instances to at most one. Ensure no replacement phrase introduces a new recurring template.

findings.acceptance_test: On a reread from Chapter 17 into Chapter 18, the first page of Chapter 18 should add new tactical information without restating the eight-hundred-meter problem, Osen's containment method, or the basic 'don't spend unless called' rule.
findings.evidence: Chapter 17 already establishes the eight-hundred-meter run; Osen's strategy of cutting every clean line west; and Vel's rule that they cannot spend early; then Chapter 18 opens by re-explaining those same facts before advancing the ordeal: snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:7993; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8027; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8117; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8136; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8148; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8156.; Chapter 17 already ends with Osen having turned the basin into a long deliberate subtraction and Vel and Kem limping toward the next cover at snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8117 and snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8121. Chapter 18 opens by resuming the same shelf; same suppression geometry; and same 'it is not enough anymore' arithmetic at snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8128; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8134; and snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8148 before the leverage actually changes much later.

evidence.excerpt: 7993: The basin floor hit like a fist made of heat and light. Red-gray bedrock, wind-scoured smooth, shimmered hard enough to hurt the eyes. Shallow erosion channels cut the floor into troughs and waist-high shelves of harder material. The threshold point lay somewhere eight hundred meters west, another patch of stripped ground blanched by sun and distance.
8027: Osen herself stayed where she was, moving only enough to remain between them and the threshold.

8117: The threshold remained off to their left, white with heat and distance. The agents kept widening around them. The tunnel mouth behind them was gone as an option. The straight line west was gone too. Osen had taken the basin Kem first saw from the ridge and turned it into something administrative: not a battle, not a chase, but a long deliberate subtraction.

8136: Vel kept her head low and looked anyway. The floor of the Scour spread west in red-gray shelves and wind-cut troughs, eight hundred meters of bad stone and no mercy in the layout. The threshold point was still a pale, unmarked patch near Osen's boots. Two descent agents were already fanning wide from the switchback. The circuiting specialist was cutting south to turn the shelf into a pocket. Farther east, two basin garrison fighters were moving for angles. Nobody was rushing. That was the part that made it hard.

8148: Vel tracked the angles in her head and did the arithmetic she had been doing since the tunnel mouth. Twenty levels between them had looked workable in the dark, in the journal, in every stupid year she had built herself around the old equation. It had to land almost all at once and almost on top of the point. That had always been the rule. Now the threshold was damped, starved, made reluctant by deliberate hands. Eight and twelve would still make a crossing if they arrived clean and close enough. Spread across eight hundred meters, it became expensive dying.
8156: "You hear me say spend, you spend. If I don't, you use your body."
8128: The shelf bought them one breath.
8134: "You can't make it work," she called. "It's not enough anymore."

findings.problem: Read back-to-back, the boundary feels like a second orientation pass rather than a new chapter movement. The reader has just lived the basin geometry and the anti-spend rule, so Chapter 18's opening recap costs momentum instead of sharpening suspense.
findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or compress the repeated basin orientation at the top of Chapter 18 and open on the first materially new tactical development: a worsened injury state, a forced spend, a visibly narrowing lane, or Sable's approach becoming actionable. The reader carries the basin rules, Osen's containment method, and the eight-hundred-meter geography from Chapter 17. Do not restate them.

findings.acceptance_test: A reader moving from snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:7847 through snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8484 should be able to chart one forward-moving clock without any reference that places the Chapter 18 chase earlier in the day than the Chapter 17 launch.
findings.evidence: The chase begins after Vel waits for amber late-afternoon light and they exit the tunnel into late-afternoon sun, but the continued pursuit in Chapter 18 abruptly says, 'By midday,' as if the clock has moved backward: snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8344.

acceptance_test.excerpt: 7847: The sun lowered. Heat changed character but did not lessen. Instead of hammering straight down it came sideways off the basin walls, baking one half of Kem's face and leaving the other dry with wind. He drank from his canteen and the water tasted like hot metal. Vel drank less. He noticed and said nothing because there was nothing to say that wasn't stupid.

8484: The sun had slid far enough west that ridge shadows striped the basin floor. Heat still leaked out of the stone, but the white noon glare was gone. Osen had spent those hours doing the same thing over and over: cutting every clean line west and forcing them sideways, keeping any level they burned too far from the threshold to matter. Vel had answered with patience, bad footing, and blood instead of spending.

evidence.excerpt: 8344: By midday the heat coming out of the rock had turned from assault to punishment. It no longer struck. It sat on the basin and made every breath feel second-used. Kem's shirt was dark down the spine. Vel's hands had started slipping on stone unless she wiped them first.

findings.problem: Because Chapter 18 reads as a direct continuation of the basin run, the midday reference breaks the physical timeline at the boundary. A careful reader has just tracked the move from lowering sun to amber light to late afternoon, so the backward jump damages confidence in the scene clock.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the time-of-day progression consistent with the late-afternoon launch. If the intent is prolonged pursuit, signal that explicitly with evening language; if the pursuit is still same-sequence, replace the midday reference with language that tracks toward sunset.

findings.acceptance_test: From the ridgeline in Chapter 17 through the threshold in Chapter 18, a reader should be able to name the same four Reserve opponents and track their functions without ever having to assume an extra agent has appeared off-page.
findings.cross_chapter_context: Ch17 establishes the Scour garrison as four opponents: Commander Osen (permanent garrison commander who has guarded the site for five years), two freshly deployed descent agents (sent for the Kem retrieval), and one circuiting specialist. Ch18 must use this same four-person roster with consistent role labels throughout the basin fight. Do not introduce additional combatants or change role designations at the chapter boundary.
findings.evidence: Chapter 17 establishes four Reserve opponents total at the Scour, but Chapter 18's opening battlefield suddenly contains two descent agents, a separate circuiting specialist, and two basin garrison fighters in addition to Osen, forcing the reader to infer extra bodies mid-pursuit: snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8136. The roster then keeps shifting between single and plural garrison fighters during the same chase: snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8178; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8272; snapshots/cycle_03/FINAL_NOVEL.md:8506.

evidence.excerpt: 8136: Vel kept her head low and looked anyway. The floor of the Scour spread west in red-gray shelves and wind-cut troughs, eight hundred meters of bad stone and no mercy in the layout. The threshold point was still a pale, unmarked patch near Osen's boots. Two descent agents were already fanning wide from the switchback. The circuiting specialist was cutting south to turn the shelf into a pocket. Farther east, two basin garrison fighters were moving for angles. Nobody was rushing. That was the part that made it hard.
8178: The east garrison fighter shifted his feet to cut the trough.

8272: They learned which rocks rang before they split. They learned which layers of sandstone would hold a hand and which would slough off in sheets. They learned that one of the garrison fighters liked low bindings at shin height and the other preferred force pulses aimed where a running body wanted its next step. They learned that Osen did not call many orders aloud because she did not need to. The Reserve were that well trained, or that well scared of getting it wrong in front of her.

8506: The basin snapped into hard focus. Northeast garrison behind a low shelf. East garrison building the suppression lane she had been feeling for half the afternoon. The specialist closing from the south with another blink loaded in the back of his posture. Osen holding back at the threshold, saving her own hand for the point.

findings.problem: The basin fight depends on the reader tracking who is closing which lane. When the headcount and role labels change at the chapter boundary, the geometry stops reading as precise pressure and starts reading as authorial handwaving.
findings.rewrite_direction: Choose one stable four-person roster and carry those labels cleanly from Chapter 17 into Chapter 18. Each opponent should keep a consistent battlefield job so later maneuvers feel like variations on known pressure, not new combatants materializing.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

Do not open a chapter by restating information the reader received at the end of the previous chapter. Each chapter must begin with forward motion, a new development, or at most a single brief orienting clause before advancing. Travel summaries, logistics recaps, and re-explanations of the prior chapter's final state are revision targets, not opening strategies.

Each chapter should contain no more than 20 hand/palm/finger references. Keep only load-bearing instances where hands perform a specific, non-substitutable action (holding a specific object, performing a skill, a hand-specific injury). Replace atmospheric and habitual hand references with varied body parts: posture, jaw, shoulders, feet, weight shift, breathing, gaze. Do not replace hand references with another single substitute that creates a new tic.

Retain at most three 'the shape of [abstract noun]' constructions across the entire manuscript. Specifically retain: (1) Ch13 line 5564's contrastive pair ('Not the shape of a hope. The shape of a requirement.') which earns its repetition through deliberate parallelism, and (2) Ch18 line 8602's 'The shape of the world started to slip' which may be defensible as literal threshold description. Replace all other instances with constructions native to their scene: physical metaphors, Ledger-vocabulary substitutions, or direct statement. No replacement phrase should introduce a new recurring template.

Do not re-describe the physical appearance of a character, object, or location that has already been established in an earlier chapter, unless the re-description serves a genuinely new narrative function: a materially changed physical state (new injury, visible deterioration), a unique institutional perspective that reframes the detail, or a plot-driving reaction to the detail. On reappearance, use a brief reference or a single new observation rather than a full visual re-inventory. Trust the reader's memory across chapter boundaries.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This is a dark comedy about a world where doing nothing is rational and doing something is self-destruction. Both the comedy and the darkness must be fully committed. Do not soften combat into choreography or spectacle — fights should hurt, cost real things, and leave damage. Do not soften the comedy into gentle satire — the absurdity of the Hoarded and their agents should be genuinely funny, sometimes cruelly so. Do not soften grief into sentimentality or revelation into epiphany. The premise demands a tonal register that holds absurdity and genuine pain in the same hand. When the material is uncomfortable — the physical reality of injury, the moral weight of exploitation, the specific grief of losing someone to a place you cannot reach — lean into specificity rather than retreating to safety. A draft that never risks anything produces a novel that never moves anyone.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in combat, injury, consequence, or emotional extremity is a craft failure. If Vel breaks ribs, the reader should feel the break — the specific wrongness of breathing with cracked bone, the way she holds herself, the pain when she forgets. If Kem is terrified, the terror should be specific and physical: hands, stomach, breathing, the precise quality of his fear. Do not abstract away from embodied experience into summary or generalization.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Sable (low) and early-book Kem (initially low, shifting to high). Uncontracted forms are reserved for deliberate emphasis, formal Hoarded speech contexts, or bureaucratic register. In casual conversation, unjustified full-form lines should be treated as suspect.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Vary chapter rhythm aggressively. Combat chapters should be compressed and physical. Discovery chapters should have more room for observation and the surprise of new information. Quiet chapters should breathe — longer scenes, more sensory detail, conversations that circle rather than drive. Intercut chapters (like Ch 11's Kem/Sable split and Ch 17's Sable intercut) should have contrasting textures between sections. No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural shape: vary scene count, dialogue density, pacing, and the ratio of action to interiority. Dark comedy chapters in the back half (Ch 12, 14, 15, 17) must commit fully to comic energy as a primary narrative mode: the absurdity of Hoarded bureaucratic language applied to human exploitation, the grim humor of fighting for your life with single-digit levels, the comedy of precision teamwork performed in emotional silence. These chapters carry genuine stakes alongside their comedy — the tonal fusion is the point, not tonal alternation.
prose_style_profile.diction: Concrete, physical, occasionally wry. Avoids both literary pretension and genre cliche. The world has its own vocabulary (Hoarded, Spent, Ledger, Stillness, the Scour, the Margins) and these terms should be used naturally, not defined in exposition. Metaphors earned from the world's own materials — spending, counting, weight, erosion — rather than imported from outside the setting. The Hoarded institutional register — bureaucratic classifications, procedural euphemism, administrative language applied to human exploitation — is a specific comic instrument in the Margins chapters. When rendering Hoarded documents, policies, or institutional speech, the language should be precise, procedural, and devastating in its indifference.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Narrator-as-comedian: comedy arises from situation and character, not from narrative commentary or winking asides to the reader
Ability-use described like video game tooltips: do not produce stat readouts or system notifications in prose
Emotional temperature permanently at maximum: not every scene is a crisis and not every internal moment is an epiphany
Generic fantasy description: landscape, architecture, and weather must be specific to this world's conditions, not recycled from the fantasy-prose commons
Competence porn: do not let characters be consistently impressive — they should fumble, miscalculate, and fail at things that are hard
Thematic underlining: the prose should not highlight its own meanings or signal when something is symbolically significant
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third-person limited, shifting with the POV character. Vel chapters are compressed and physical — the prose should feel like it is counting. Kem chapters are more expansive and curious — the prose has more room for observation and surprise. Sable chapters are structured and institutional, loosening as she changes. The POV break in Ch 13–14 (two consecutive Kem chapters) should make Vel's absence felt: the reader is locked inside Kem's betrayed perspective, and the prose should reflect the claustrophobia of a mind cycling through anger without access to the other person's interiority. The distance should be intimate enough to access characters' internal arithmetic but not so close it becomes stream-of-consciousness.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied by chapter texture and scene pressure. Short declarative sentences in combat. Longer, conditional, clause-heavy sentences in reflection. Comma-heavy run-ons in panic or exhilaration. The Ledger's arithmetic should bleed into prose rhythm during ability-use moments — characters counting, weighing, calculating. Quiet chapters should breathe. Action chapters should compress. Two consecutive chapters should not share the same dominant rhythm.
sensory_bias: proprioceptive awareness — the body's internal sense of position, strain, damage, and capacity
physical weight and effort — characters feel gravity, distance, fatigue
the Ledger's feedback — a character's awareness of their remaining levels as a persistent background sensation
environmental specificity — terrain, weather, architecture rendered through what a body encounters
